item.title: the source code made it hurt even more
item.content:encoded: +1. Goodbye, Josh.

item.title: Re: Seems a bit more bittersweet than sad
item.content:encoded: Damn

item.title: Re: Seems a bit more bittersweet than sad
item.content:encoded: The implication is that the other entity in there with him is blocking communications.

item.title: Re: Seems a bit more bittersweet than sad
item.content:encoded: Yeah I saw the part where some researcher(s) tried to contact him but couldn't get through. That's where the bitter really comes in.

item.title: Re: Seems a bit more bittersweet than sad
item.content:encoded:
Spoiler
You're not reading into it enough. Look at the source code.

[YOUR TEXT]

item.title: Seems a bit more bittersweet than sad
item.content:encoded: I might be reading too much into this, but the last part sounds like he still managed to find someone even while trapped in a virtual database. And wherever he's going, he'll be spending it with her.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. Scroll to the bottom of the page where there are links started with "Edit"
  2. One of these is "+Options"; click that.
  3. One of the new links that appears is "Page Source"; Click that
  4. Now you can scroll down and read the page source.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I used developer tools to read it, it was more difficult, but I found it. I didn't know how to view the page source.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Uh.. Wow..

I have like, an irrational spot in me for stuff like this. It really hits me where it hurts…

Goodbye, Josh…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
thought 1762 was the only sad skip

/maniacal laughter

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Welp, and thought 1762 was the only sad skip i would ever see
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Goodbye, Josh."

I choked up a little bit. +1 for my favourite article on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*slow clap*

That was very good Fae. Very, very good. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this one. The writing is good, and it uses a small format screw to continue the story of it without fully relying on it.

Here's just one thing that bothers me about this document; if he edits the document, then why is it that when he…"left," he didn't change his special containment procedures to that the anomaly was neutralized, or uncontained, or whatever it would be?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the best irony is this - he's the one who expressed the containment procedure, which has clearly been ignored.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's size-zero text there? I can't find it o_O

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm no-voting on this basis. I've enjoyed some size-zero trickery in the past, but that was little creepy bites of flavor (like in ███.) This just seems like good parts of the article that are hidden for no reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the little tragedies. This tugs on all those strings of loneliness and abandonment. The fact that he (and the majority of readers) doesn't realize they are trying to contact him.

BTW guys, the hint you asked for is the comment on version 0: "read the source code." IF (and only if) you want to drop a larger hint, make the first couple of words (before whatever is interfering catches on) size 1. Still unreadable at any browser zoom, but more likely to make the reader go to the source and see "what the heck that was."

+1 for beautifully tragic story. Goodbye, Josh.

I am waiting eagerly for the hinted-at pre/sequel(s), or maybe a sister SCP?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I first started reading this, I got excited to see how one could try to communicate emotionally through the clinical SCP article format. When I realized this would be sidestepped through the use of notes in the addendum, I suddenly became less interested. I have a particular disdain for 732, so its inclusion in this didn't help matters, quite frankly. The researcher's storyline didn't wrap up very satisfyingly for me, either.

As a general thing, I'm okay with easter eggs in the source as long as the article may be fully understood without discovering it. I don't feel that's the case here, and with no indication of it whatsoever in the article itself, it bothers me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it, but the last note didn't work for me, even with the source code. It doesn't really fit with the loneliness of the rest of the article when he just goes "screw you guys, I'm goin' home." Neutral voting because I liked the fanfic plague interaction, and the general idea up to the end of the piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow this was great! +1. I can see why other people would find it laborious with the format wonkery, but I love that stuff. I think it stands on its own without the size 0, but the size 0 adds such a great extra layer, making it more tragic. And I eagerly look forward to any further tales of Josh and the mysterious lady he left with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Apologies for not commenting or responding to your PM on this earlier, I was pretty busy. However I enjoyed this quite a lot and I'm excited to see what MacLeod is cooking up. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unless the "more" is placed within the article, it's not going to change my vote. I might enjoy a tale or something, but I generally vote those based on their own merits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kinda feel like I need to expand on my comment…

It's not so much the source code gimmick as the fact that you wouldn't know it's there unless you habitually check page source or copy out articles into notepad (or read comments). If there were a clue in the article, my opinion might be different.

I think what I was trying to say was more "jump through hoops I didn't know were there to get the whole story…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Completely agree. The story stands on its own, but the source code does so much more. Anything that would allude us to the source code additions would have been nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the story. Agreeing that the bonus content is cool, but I would never have thought to check the source code if I hadn't read the comments, and that a hint about that would be great. (Doesn't have to be a very blatant hint- just knowing that there was hidden text on the page other than the black boxes would work.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm just going to point out that on previous post it was hinted there's going to be more about what he found. I'm not trying to change your mind about this particular SCP or anything, just letting you know in case you're interested.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for the creative concept. Perhaps a clue or two more to let readers know there's more than their seeing might be nice. I read the whole thing and didn't realize there was more until I got into the comments here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No-vote. Initially, I felt this was too much like 2710. Then I highlighted the article when it was suggested to do so, and was hooked. But the highlighted censor bars don't reveal anything important, which was disappointing. And while the source code gimmick added some depth, or at least keeps the Foundation from looking like cold assholes, it's just that, a gimmick, and I don't feel like it goes far enough. I was really expecting some indication of whoever it was he found. So I'm gonna go with "just a pile of gimmicks" on this one, sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted before I knew about the notes in the source. I don't like it either, but it seems petty to downvote based on something that I didn't even need to know about.

I don't see how a reader is supposed to know it is there, or what it is supposed to represent in-universe (I seriously doubt that the Foundation uses a wiki internally).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wavering no-vote changed to cheerful upvote on reading the source code. Clever, bittersweet, nifty.
Tiny tiny niggle, could someone please change 'rouge' to 'rogue'? It's just a personal bugbear, but I'm so grotesquely untechy that I'll probably brick the whole article somehow if I try.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reluctant downvote on a pet peeve issue. If I have to jump through hoops (copy/paste the article into notepad or read the source) to get the whole story, it annoys me significantly.

That said, it works here - I just can't get behind it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes and no.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really missed seeing those black box with something actually written on them and the 0% thing (hope you don't mind me using the code later on). IMO they add a special touch to the article.

Also, I wonder if SCP-000 will be making an appearance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
/me blows into tissue

Go on! Take it! Take my upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Option 2.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is this because MacLeod had the idea for that ending or he has something in the works/already out that is a… Something in the system?

Edit: also, +1, I enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just… just awesome. Really awesome.

+1. Sad I can't +more

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ask MacLeod.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cute. One does wonder who he found in there…

item.title: Genius
item.content:encoded: I love the use of the source code to drive the story! Brilliant! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bottom of the page, click "+Options" then click "Page Source"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Let's say I don't know how to read the source code…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Read the source code.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why did they ignore him? Did nobody notice the 2717 slot? Was he the victim of compartmentalization?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I likey

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Thank you to Drewbear, RubyPhoenix, Moose, thekillerax, Macleod, and EldritchCyanide for reviewing this.

Read the source code.
